questions.question: Order the typical training progression from start to overfitting.
items: Large gap between training and validation performance
Both training and validation loss are high
Validation loss starts increasing
Both training and validation loss decrease together
Training loss keeps dropping but validation loss plateaus
correct_order: Both training and validation loss are high
Both training and validation loss decrease together
Training loss keeps dropping but validation loss plateaus
Validation loss starts increasing
Large gap between training and validation performance
questions.explanation: Training starts with high loss on both sets (underfitting). Then both improve together as the model learns useful patterns. Eventually training loss keeps improving but validation stalls. Then validation loss starts rising, and finally there is a large gap indicating clear overfitting.

questions.question: Order the steps for applying early stopping.
items: Restore the model weights from the best checkpoint
Stop when validation loss has not improved for several epochs
Monitor validation loss after each epoch
Split data into training and validation sets
Train the model
correct_order: Split data into training and validation sets
Train the model
Monitor validation loss after each epoch
Stop when validation loss has not improved for several epochs
Restore the model weights from the best checkpoint
questions.explanation: First you need a validation set to monitor. Then you train the model while checking validation loss each epoch. When validation loss stops improving for a set number of epochs (patience), you stop training and restore the model to the point where validation loss was lowest.

questions.question: Order these from underfitting (most) to overfitting (most).
items: A model with strong regularization that cannot learn patterns
A model with no regularization trained for many epochs
A well-tuned model with moderate regularization
A very simple model on complex data
correct_order: A very simple model on complex data
A model with strong regularization that cannot learn patterns
A well-tuned model with moderate regularization
A model with no regularization trained for many epochs
questions.explanation: A very simple model on complex data underfits the most. Strong regularization also constrains the model toward underfitting. Moderate regularization achieves a good balance. No regularization with long training leads to the most overfitting.

questions.question: Order the effect of increasing regularization strength from low to high.
items: Model underfits, both training and validation accuracy are low
Training accuracy drops slightly, validation accuracy improves
Barely any effect, model may still overfit
Good balance between training fit and generalization
correct_order: Barely any effect, model may still overfit
Training accuracy drops slightly, validation accuracy improves
Good balance between training fit and generalization
Model underfits, both training and validation accuracy are low
questions.explanation: With very low regularization, the effect is minimal. As it increases, training accuracy drops slightly but validation improves. At the right level, there is a good balance. Too much regularization over-constrains the model, causing underfitting with poor performance on both sets.

questions.question: Order the steps of how dropout works during a training forward pass.
items: Pass the result to the next layer
Randomly select which neurons to turn off
Compute the layer's activations normally
Set the selected neurons to zero
correct_order: Compute the layer's activations normally
Randomly select which neurons to turn off
Set the selected neurons to zero
Pass the result to the next layer
questions.explanation: During a forward pass with dropout, the layer first computes its activations as normal. Then a random subset of neurons is selected to be dropped. Those neurons are set to zero. Finally, the remaining activations are passed to the next layer.

questions.question: Order these data augmentation techniques from simplest to most complex.
items: Combining multiple augmentations randomly
Random rotation by small angles
Horizontal flipping
Color jittering with brightness and contrast changes
correct_order: Horizontal flipping
Random rotation by small angles
Color jittering with brightness and contrast changes
Combining multiple augmentations randomly
questions.explanation: Horizontal flipping is the simplest -- just mirror the image. Random rotation requires choosing an angle and interpolating pixels. Color jittering involves adjusting multiple color properties. Combining multiple augmentations randomly is the most complex as it layers several transformations together.

questions.question: Order these models from highest bias (simplest) to lowest bias (most complex).
items: Deep neural network with many layers
Shallow network with heavy dropout
Linear model
Medium network with moderate regularization
correct_order: Linear model
Shallow network with heavy dropout
Medium network with moderate regularization
Deep neural network with many layers
questions.explanation: A linear model has the highest bias because it can only learn linear relationships. A shallow network with heavy dropout is also quite constrained. A medium network with moderate regularization has more capacity. A deep network with many layers has the lowest bias and highest capacity to learn complex patterns.

questions.question: Order the batch normalization steps.
items: Use the result as input to the next layer
Normalize using the mean and variance
Compute the mean of the mini-batch
Apply learnable scale and shift parameters
Compute the variance of the mini-batch
correct_order: Compute the mean of the mini-batch
Compute the variance of the mini-batch
Normalize using the mean and variance
Apply learnable scale and shift parameters
Use the result as input to the next layer
questions.explanation: Batch normalization first computes the mini-batch mean, then the variance. It normalizes the activations to have zero mean and unit variance. Learnable scale and shift parameters are applied so the network can adjust the distribution if needed. The result is then passed forward.

questions.question: Order these strategies for fixing overfitting, from first to try to last resort.
items: Collect more real training data
Apply data augmentation
Add L2 regularization or dropout
Diagnose the problem using learning curves
correct_order: Diagnose the problem using learning curves
Add L2 regularization or dropout
Apply data augmentation
Collect more real training data
questions.explanation: First, always diagnose the problem by looking at learning curves to confirm overfitting. Then try simple regularization like L2 or dropout since they are easy to add. Next, try data augmentation to increase training diversity. Collecting more real data is effective but typically the most time-consuming and expensive approach.

questions.question: Order the relationship between model complexity and performance.
items: Optimal complexity: best generalization to new data
Very high complexity: overfitting, poor test performance
Very low complexity: underfitting, poor on all data
Moderate complexity: improving test performance
correct_order: Very low complexity: underfitting, poor on all data
Moderate complexity: improving test performance
Optimal complexity: best generalization to new data
Very high complexity: overfitting, poor test performance
questions.explanation: As model complexity increases from very low, the model moves from underfitting through improving performance to an optimal point with the best generalization. Beyond that optimal point, adding more complexity leads to overfitting where test performance degrades even though training performance continues to improve.
